items.title: Green stem: intact cortex
items.contentHtml:
Greenstick fracture: intact convex cortex

  • The greenstick fracture is characterized by an interruption of the cortex on the concave side (compression), while the convex cortex remains intact, which gives it inherent stability.
  • It is an incomplete fracture typical of immature bone, with a thick periosteum that acts as a hinge, limiting the displacement.
  • The radiograph shows a fracture line that does not cross the entire diaphysis, with angulation on the opposite side to the broken cortex.
  • The main differential diagnosis is buncle fracture (torus), which presents cortical bulging without a clear fracture line.

items.foco: Compression and angulation mechanism in immature bone
items.title: Mechanism: compression and angulation
items.contentHtml:
Compression and angulation mechanism in immature bone

  • The typical mechanism is an axial compression force combined with angulation on a bone with high collagen content and porosity, which fails first on the side subjected to compression.
  • In children, the periosteum is thick and resistant, which allows the convex cortex to bend without breaking, generating the “stem” image green.”
  • The angulation occurs because the bone buckles at the point of impact, and the intact cortex prevents complete spontaneous reduction, sometimes requiring manipulation.
  • This mechanism is different from that of the complete fracture in the adult, where both cortices are broken by simultaneous traction and compression.

items.foco: Paronychia: infection of the proximal nail fold
items.title: Paronychia: definition
items.contentHtml:
Paronychia: infection of the proximal nail fold

  • Paronychia is an acute or chronic infection of the proximal nail fold (eponychium) and lateral, which can extend to the subungual space.
  • It is classified as acute (duration <6 weeks, usually bacterial) and chronic (>6 weeks, associated with irritants and fungi).
  • Clinically it presents with erythema, edema, pain and, in advanced stages, abscess formation visible as a yellowish spot under the cuticle.
  • The diagnosis is clinical; Routine imaging or cultures are not required, except in recurrent or immunocompromised cases.

items.foco: Most common causative agent: Staphylococcus aureus
items.title: Causative agent: Staphylococcus aureus
items.contentHtml:
Most common causative agent: Staphylococcus aureus

  • In acute paronychia, the most common causative agent is Staphylococcus aureus, followed by beta-hemolytic and oral anaerobic streptococci (in sucking or biting).
  • In chronic paronychia, the main pathogen is Candida albicans, often associated with contact dermatitis or exposure to moisture.
  • In immunocompromised patients or patients with diabetes mellitus, there may be polymicrobial infection including gram-negatives such as Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
  • Initial empirical treatment should cover Staphylococcus aureus, using topical antibiotics such as mupirocin or acid. fusidic; If there is an abscess, drainage is a priority.

items.foco: Green stem: immobilization with plaster for 3-4 weeks
items.title: Green stem: immobilization
items.contentHtml:
Greenstick: cast immobilization for 3-4 weeks

  • Most greenstick fractures with mild angulation (<20° in forearm) are treated with cast immobilization for 3-4 weeks, without the need for reduction.
  • The cast should include the joint proximal and distal to the fracture site (e.g. forearm: brachial or antebrachial cast depending on location).
  • The risk of compartment syndrome should be monitored in the first 24-48 hours, especially if the cast is circular.
  • Bone consolidation in children is rapid; At 3 weeks there is usually enough callus to remove the immobilization, with radiographic control at 4 weeks.

items.foco: Acute paronychia: surgical drainage if there is an abscess
items.title: Acute paronychia: surgical drainage
items.contentHtml:
Acute paronychia: surgical drainage if there is an abscess

  • When there is abscess (fluctuation or yellowish spot), the treatment of choice is surgical drainage through an incision of the proximal nail fold.
  • The technique consists of lifting the eponychium with a number 11 scalpel blade, draining the pus and placing a gauze wick or Penrose drainage for 24-48 hours.
  • The nail bed should not be incised directly (risk of permanent deformity); The incision is parallel to the edge of the nail.
  • It is complemented with topical antibiotic (mupirocin) and, in extensive or immunocompromised cases, oral antibiotic such as cephalexin 500 mg every 6 hours for 7 days.

items.foco: Green stem: risk of residual angular deformity
items.title: Green stem: residual angular deformity
items.contentHtml:
Greenstick: risk of residual angular deformity

  • Residual angular deformity is a late complication of greenstick fractures that are poorly reduced or with insufficient immobilization.
  • The risk is greater in fractures of the forearm (radius and ulna) when the angulation exceeds 20°, since bone remodeling may not completely correct the axis.
  • In children Under 8 years of age, the potential for remodeling is high (up to 30° in the plane of movement), but in over 10 years of age the correction capacity decreases.
  • Radiographic follow-up is recommended at 2 and 4 weeks to evaluate alignment; If angulation >15° persists in older children, consider corrective osteotomy.

items.foco: Chronic paronychia: associated with Candida albicans
items.title: Chronic paronychia: Candida albicans
items.contentHtml:
Chronic paronychia: associated with Candida albicans

  • Chronic paronychia (>6 weeks) is frequently associated with Candida albicans, especially in patients with chronic exposure to humidity (dishwashers, cooks, swimmers).
  • Clinically it presents with erythema, mild edema, loss of the cuticle and onycholysis, without formation of abscess.
  • The diagnosis is confirmed with mycological culture of the nail fold or direct examination with potassium hydroxide (KOH) that shows pseudohyphae.
  • Treatment includes avoiding humidity, applying topical antifungal such as clotrimazole or ketoconazole cream 2 times a day for 4-8 weeks; in resistant cases, oral itraconazole 200 mg/day for 6 weeks.

items.foco: Labrum fracture: type of fracture in children
items.title: Torus fracture
items.contentHtml:
Labrum fracture: type of fracture in children

  • The buncle fracture (torus) is a compression fracture of the metaphysis of the immature bone, where the cortex folds or bulges without a visible fracture line.
  • It is the most common fracture in children under 10 years of age, typically in the distal radius after a fall with the hand. extended.
  • Radiographically, a cortical bulging is observed on the compression side, without involvement of the opposite cortex or displacement.
  • Treatment is symptomatic: immobilization with a plaster splint for 2-3 weeks; It does not require reduction and has an excellent prognosis with complete remodeling.

items.foco: Paronychia: initial treatment with topical antibiotic
items.title: Paronychia: initial topical antibiotic
items.contentHtml:
Paronychia: initial treatment with topical antibiotic

  • In acute paronychia without abscess, the initial treatment is topical antibiotic such as mupirocin 2% or fusidic acid 2%, applied 3-4 times a day to the affected fold.
  • Warm water baths with antiseptic soap (chlorhexidine) for 15 minutes, 3 times a day, to facilitate spontaneous drainage.
  • If there is no improvement in 48 hours or fluctuation appears, surgical drainage should be indicated; Oral antibiotic therapy is reserved for extensive cellulitis or immunocompromise.
  • In chronic paronychia, the initial treatment is topical antifungal (clotrimazole) and hygiene measures; Topical antibiotics are not indicated.

items.foco: Green stem: does not require reduction if slight angulation
items.title: Green stem: slight reduction in angulation
items.contentHtml:
Green stem: no reduction required if slight angulation

  • In green stem fractures with mild angulation (<20° in forearm, <15° in humerus), no reduction is required, only cast immobilization.
  • The intact cortex provides sufficient stability to maintain alignment during consolidation; bone remodeling will correct residual angulation in young children.
  • If the angulation exceeds these thresholds or there is rotation, closed reduction should be performed under sedation, reversing the fracture mechanism (pressure on the convexity).
  • Open reduction is rarely necessary; It is indicated only if there is soft tissue entrapment or failure of closed reduction.

items.foco: Paronychia: avoid incision in the nail bed
items.title: Paronychia: avoid incision in the nail bed
items.contentHtml:
Paronychia: avoid incision in the nail bed

  • When performing surgical drainage of a paronychia, one should avoid incision in the nail bed (under the nail) because it can cause permanent deformity of the nail (onychocryptosis, onychogryphosis).
  • The correct incision is made in the fold. proximal nail bed (eponychium), parallel to the edge of the nail, elevating the cuticle with the scalpel blade.
  • If the abscess extends under the nail (subungual paronychia), partial nail resection (avulsion) may be required to drain adequately, but the bed is never incised.
  • The nail bed is the area responsible for the growth of the nail; Any iatrogenic injury can generate grooves, ridges or loss of the nail.
